content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G BROWN JR., Governor

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 K STREET
SACRAMENTO 95814
(916) 322-5660

November 4, 1975

Legislative Counsel of California
3021 State Capitol
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Attention: George H. Murphy

Re: Request for Opinion
    No. 75-195

Dear Mr. Murphy:

     Thank you for your letter of October 17, 1975, re-
questing an opinion concerning mass mailing privileges of
elected State officers. Since your letter raises no sub-
stantial question involving an interpretation of the Act,
no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the
following informal comments will be helpful.

     The prohibition contained in Government Code Section
89001 applies only after the time that the officer has
filed a declaration of candidacy. When the officer is
elected, re-elected or otherwise ceases to be a declared
candidate for office, the mass mailing privilege is re-
stored.

     If you have any questions, please contact Kenneth
Finney, Director of the Legal Division.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                        Chairman, for the Commission

DHL:pvp

Legislative Counsel
of California

GEORGE H. MURPHY

Sacramento, California
October 17, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Commissioners:

On behalf of Senator James R. Mills, I
request your opinion pursuant to Section 83114 of
the Government Code on the following question:

Does the prohibition of Section 89001
against an elected state officer sending a
mass mailing at public expense after filing
a declaration of candidacy for elective
office, continue to apply after the officer
is elected, reelected, or otherwise ceases
to be a declared candidate?

We note that the text of Section 89001 con-
tains no language expressly permitting the restoration
of mass mailing privileges after the elected state
officer is elected, reelected, or otherwise ceases to
be a declared candidate for any office. In fact, a
strict interpretation of the language may prevent, for
all time, legislators who have filed for other elective
office, such as member of a county board of supervisors,
from using future legislative mass mailing privileges.

Very truly yours,

George H. Murphy
Legislative Counsel

By: [illegible]
Michael J. Ferraian
Deputy Legislative Counsel

MJF:cq
